summary: Julia Evans compiles a comprehensive list of programming playgrounds — interactive browser-based tools for experimenting with code, data formats, and tools without any local setup. The list was crowdsourced from Mastodon and covers everything from compilers and shaders to DNS, SQL, regex, and dozens of programming languages. She organizes them into categories including general tools, data formats, and programming languages (both official and unofficial playgrounds). The post is primarily a curated reference resource rather than an argumentative piece, reflecting her enthusiasm for interactive learning tools.
key_points: Programming playgrounds exist for an enormous range of tools and technologies, from compilers (Godbolt) to Arduino (Wokwi) to DNS (Mess With DNS)
Many programming languages now offer official browser-based playgrounds, including Go, Rust, Ruby, TypeScript, Haskell, and others
Data format playgrounds for things like floating point, Unicode, ASN.1 certificates, and cron expressions are valuable but less well-known
The list was crowdsourced from Mastodon, demonstrating collaborative knowledge-sharing in the developer community
money_quotes: I really like using (and making!) programming playgrounds, and I got thinking the other day about how I didn't have a great list of playgrounds to refer to.
key_insight: The breadth of available programming playgrounds — from regex testers to DNS tools to language REPLs — makes interactive experimentation accessible for nearly every technical concept, and curating them into a single reference is itself a valuable contribution.
